message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it from the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Find the code path/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Only survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

X-1: HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK tier enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)
File: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:414
Description: The two reasoning tiers are parallel mechanisms that both must bound a single LLM call's cost/duration. BULK got a hard generation cap: `_bulk_complete` always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 99, 368), and the docstring at lines 95-99 explicitly argues this is 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a backend call already blocked in generation. HIGH_STAKES (`_high_stakes_complete`, lines 414-503) builds `ClaudeAgentOptions` with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — there is no `max_tokens`/output bound passed at all. The result is asymmetric: a runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock, but a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` wall-clock. For the tier explicitly described as 'gated, traced' and the one that consumes the fixed-cost subscription budget, the cheaper/safer of the two guards (a bounded output) was applied only to the local tier. The same reasoning the BULK docstring uses ('the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in the backend') applies to the subscription subprocess.
PoC: Set ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=2048 (default). A BULK call cannot emit more than 2048 tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt that elicits a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and 300s of a bounded-pool worker slot, where BULK would have stopped at 2048 tokens well inside its window. grep for `num_predict` shows it only in `_bulk_complete`; grep for `max_tokens` in seam.py returns nothing.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

115	
116	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
117	# Credential scrubbing (ADR-0003 / no metered API) — pure, forward-proof
118	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
119	def is_metered_anthropic_credential(key: str) -> bool:
120	    """True for any env var the Anthropic SDK would read as a METERED credential.
121	
122	    Matches the *shape* — any ``ANTHROPIC_*`` var naming a ``KEY`` or ``TOKEN`` — rather than
123	    a static list, so a newly-added metered var cannot silently slip through. The subscription
124	    ``CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`` does not start with ``ANTHROPIC_`` and is deliberately kept.
125	
126	    The match is case-insensitive (pass 3, #F-003): a metered key set under a non-uppercase name
127	    (``anthropic_api_key``) must scrub too — the AWS_/GOOGLE_ checks already uppercase, so this
128	    closes the one credential path that did not.
129	    """
130	    ku = key.upper()
131	    return ku.startswith("ANTHROPIC_") and ("KEY" in ku or "TOKEN" in ku)
132	
133	
134	# Env vars that — beyond metered credentials — would redirect the subprocess OFF-BOX or onto a
135	# metered provider, defeating ADR-0003 / "no metered API". The claude CLI honours all of these.
136	_UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS = frozenset(
137	    {
138	        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK",
139	        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX",
140	        "CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER",
141	        "GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS",
142	        # TLS-trust / egress-interception knobs the Node-based CLI honours: a custom CA or disabled
143	        # cert verification enables a transparent off-box MITM proxy (pass 4, #G-018).
144	        "NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS",
145	        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED",
146	    }
147	)
148	
149	
150	def is_unsafe_subprocess_var(key: str) -> bool:
151	    """True for any env var that must not reach the high-stakes subprocess (#101).
152	
153	    Covers metered credentials, off-box/metered-provider redirects, proxies (which redirect
154	    egress), cloud-provider credentials (AWS_*/GOOGLE_*), and TLS-trust overrides. The ENTIRE
155	    ``ANTHROPIC_*`` namespace is scrubbed (#G-018): any such var configures the SDK's endpoint /
156	    auth / headers and could redirect off-box or onto a metered provider — the subscription token
157	    is ``CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`` (no ``ANTHROPIC_`` prefix) and is deliberately NOT matched.
158	    """
159	    ku = key.upper()
160	    return (
161	        ku.startswith(
162	            "ANTHROPIC_"
163	        )  # whole namespace: endpoint/auth/headers/custom-headers (#G-018)
164	        or ku in _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS
165	        or ku.endswith("_PROXY")  # HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any case)
166	        or ku.startswith("AWS_")
167	        or ku.startswith("GOOGLE_")
168	    )
169	
170	
171	def scrubbed_env() -> dict[str, str]:
172	    """The process env hardened for the high-stakes subprocess (ADR-0003 / no metered API).
173	
174	    Every unsafe var (:func:`is_unsafe_subprocess_var`) is **overwritten to empty**, not merely
175	    omitted. The claude-agent-sdk builds the child env as ``{**os.environ, **options.env}`` — it
176	    *merges* over the inherited environment — so a dropped key would silently fall through to its
177	    inherited value (the metered key / off-box redirect would survive). Setting it to ``""`` in
178	    the dict we hand over guarantees the merge overwrites the inherited value (#101). The
179	    subscription OAuth token is preserved so the fixed-cost path still authenticates.
180	    """
181	    env = dict(os.environ)
182	    for k in list(env):
183	        if is_unsafe_subprocess_var(k):
184	            env[k] = ""
185	    return env
186	
187	
188	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
189	# Bounded timeout pool with saturation guard
190	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
191	_POOL_WORKERS = 8
192	_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_POOL_WORKERS, thread_name_prefix="reasoning-timeout")
193	# Shut the pool down at interpreter exit (#F-043). wait=False avoids blocking exit on in-flight
194	# backend calls (already bounded by _with_timeout); without this, module globals can be torn down
195	# while a worker is mid-call, leaving behavior undefined. Mirrors checkpoint.py's atexit cleanup.
196	atexit.register(_pool.shutdown, wait=False)
197	_inflight = 0
198	_inflight_lock = threading.Lock()
199	
200	
201	def _release(future: Any) -> None:
202	    # Idempotent per future (#F-032 follow-up): decrement _inflight AT MOST ONCE for a given
203	    # future. The done-callback path and the interrupt-window fallback in _submit_bounded can BOTH
204	    # reach _release for the same future when add_done_callback appended the callback and then was
205	    # interrupted before returning — without this guard that double-decrements and over-releases a
206	    # slot (the inverse of the leak being fixed). A per-future flag set under the lock makes the
207	    # "_release runs at most once" claim true rather than aspirational.
208	    global _inflight
209	    with _inflight_lock:

447	    async def _drain() -> None:
448	        # The CLI/SDK signals rate windows and API failures by COMPLETING the stream
449	        # (a RateLimitEvent, or a terminal ResultMessage with is_error) — NOT by raising.
450	        # We must inspect the stream, not just catch exceptions (issue #R4).
451	        #
452	        # Inner cancellation (#127): wrap the stream in anyio.fail_after so a hung subprocess is
453	        # actually CANCELLED (the cancellation propagates into query() and tears down the child),
454	        # freeing the bounded-pool worker. The outer _with_timeout only raises in the caller; it
455	        # cannot cancel this thread, so without this a hung HIGH_STAKES call would leak its slot
456	        # permanently. Fires before the outer wall-clock (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30).
457	        nonlocal result_error
458	        with anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT):
459	            await _drain_stream()
460	
461	    async def _drain_stream() -> None:
462	        nonlocal result_error
463	        async for msg in query(prompt=prompt, options=options):
464	            if type(msg).__name__ == "RateLimitEvent":
465	                rl = getattr(getattr(msg, "rate_limit_info", None), "status", None)
466	                if rl == "rejected":
467	                    result_error = {"status": 429, "detail": "RateLimitEvent rejected"}
468	                continue
469	            if getattr(msg, "status", None) == "rejected":
470	                result_error = {"status": 429, "detail": "rejected"}
471	                continue
472	            if isinstance(msg, AssistantMessage):
473	                for block in getattr(msg, "content", []) or []:
474	                    if isinstance(block, TextBlock):
475	                        parts.append(block.text)
476	            elif isinstance(msg, ResultMessage):
477	                # Do not let a trailing ResultMessage clobber an already-captured 429.
478	                if getattr(msg, "is_error", False) and not (
479	                    result_error and result_error.get("status") == 429
480	                ):
481	                    result_error = {
482	                        "status": getattr(msg, "api_error_status", None),
483	                        "detail": getattr(msg, "errors", None) or getattr(msg, "stop_reason", None),
484	                    }
485	
486	    try:
487	        anyio.run(_drain)
488	    except Exception as exc:
489	        raise ReasoningError(f"high-stakes tier call failed: {exc}") from exc
490	
491	    if result_error is not None:
492	        status = result_error.get("status")
493	        detail = result_error.get("detail") or status
494	        if status in (429, 529):
495	            raise RateLimitError(f"high-stakes tier rate window ({status}): {detail}")
496	        if status in (401, 403):
497	            raise PermanentReasoningError(f"high-stakes tier auth failed ({status}): {detail}")
498	        raise ReasoningError(f"high-stakes tier reported an error (status={status}): {detail}")
499	
500	    text = "".join(parts).strip()
501	    if not text:  # empty frontier reply is not a valid result — retry (issue #R5)
502	        raise ReasoningError("high-stakes tier returned an empty reply")
503	    return text

content.text: The code confirms the structural asymmetry. Let me check whether `ClaudeAgentOptions` even supports a `max_tokens`/output-bound parameter — if the SDK provides no such knob, the finding's premise (that the cap "was applied only to the local tier") would be moot because there's no equivalent to apply.

content.text:
Critical evidence. The SDK's `ClaudeAgentOptions` (line 1634) exposes:
- `max_turns: int | None = None` (line 1708) — caps agentic turns, not output length per call
- `max_thinking_tokens: int | None = None` (line 1906) — caps thinking/reasoning tokens

There is no `max_tokens` / `max_output_tokens` field on `ClaudeAgentOptions`. The `maxTokens: int` at line 772 and `maxTurns` at line 98 belong to other classes. Let me confirm what classes those belong to, and check `max_thinking_tokens` semantics — whether it bounds output.

This is directly relevant: the finding claims HIGH_STAKES has "NO max-output-tokens equivalent" and only wall-clock. But the SDK exposes `max_budget_usd` AND `max_turns` — both of which would bound a runaway generation independent of wall-clock. The question is whether the *project* uses them. It doesn't (per the options block at line 435-442). This fully resolves the question. The findings claim is technically accurate about the code, but let me assess the refutation angle precisely.

The finding asserts an **asymmetry**: BULK has a bounded output via `num_predict`, HIGH_STAKES has none and is only bounded by wall-clock. Let me verify the two genuine refutation candidates:

1. **Is there actually a per-call output bound the SDK offers that the seam ignores?** The SDK has no `max_tokens`/`max_output_tokens` (output-completion-token cap) on `ClaudeAgentOptions` at all. `max_turns` bounds agentic turns (irrelevant here — single query). `max_thinking_tokens` bounds thinking, not output. `max_budget_usd` bounds cost. So a *direct* `num_predict` equivalent (output completion token cap) **does not exist** in this SDK. The finding's specific PoC ("grep for max_tokens returns nothing") is correct but the parallel mechanism it implies (`max_tokens`) simply isn't available.

2. **Is the runaway actually bounded another way?** Let me check the outer timeout and whether `_drain` truly cancels the subprocess.

content.text:
Now I have the complete picture. Let me evaluate the refutation honestly.

**The finding's factual claims are all verified as true:**
- BULK sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` always (line 368).
- HIGH_STAKES's `ClaudeAgentOptions` (lines 435-442) sets no output/cost/turn bound at all.
- HIGH_STAKES is bounded only by `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (inner, line 458) and `_with_timeout(..., HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)` (outer, line 550) — both wall-clock.
- `grep max_tokens` → nothing; `num_predict` only in BULK.

**Refutation attempts:**

1. *"The SDK has no output-token cap, so there's nothing to apply"* — Partially mitigates the literal `max_tokens` framing, but **does not moot the finding**. The SDK DOES expose `max_budget_usd` (line 1714, "query will stop if this budget is exceeded") and `max_turns` — direct, available cost/runaway guards that bound a HIGH_STAKES call independent of wall-clock, and neither is set. So the asymmetry the finding describes (BULK has a hard generation bound, HIGH_STAKES has only wall-clock) holds, and an analogous guard was in fact available and unused.

2. *"Wall-clock fully bounds it anyway"* — This is precisely the argument the BULK docstring (lines 95-99) rejects: a backend call blocked in generation isn't interruptible by wall-clock alone; the bound is what guarantees termination. The `anyio.fail_after` does cancel the subprocess, but only after the full timeout elapses, so the runaway consumes the entire 300s window + a pool slot — exactly the asymmetric cost the finding describes. Wall-clock is a duration bound, not a cost/length bound.

3. *Is there a test or contract enforcing a HIGH_STAKES output bound?* — No `max_tokens`/`max_budget`/`max_turns` appears anywhere in seam.py; nothing enforces it.

I cannot disprove the finding. The asymmetry is real, the wall-clock-only bound is exactly what the BULK docstring argues is insufficient as the sole guard, and an applicable cost/runaway guard (`max_budget_usd`/`max_turns`) exists in the pinned SDK but is unused.

One caveat worth noting in reasoning: the severity is somewhat tempered because HIGH_STAKES *is* terminated (cancelled subprocess) at 300s, so it's not unbounded — it's a weaker bound, not no bound. But the finding explicitly acknowledges this (it says "bounded only by wall-clock") and argues the asymmetry/quality-of-guard point, which stands.

input.reasoning:
I could not disprove the finding; all its factual claims verify against the actual code.

Verified facts (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py):
- BULK: `_bulk_complete` always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 368; default 2048, line 99), and the docstring lines 95-99 explicitly argue this output bound is "the real guard" because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a backend call already blocked in generation.
- HIGH_STAKES: `_high_stakes_complete` builds `ClaudeAgentOptions(system_prompt, model, permission_mode, setting_sources, allowed_tools, env)` (lines 435-442) with NO output/cost/turn bound of any kind. Its only guards are wall-clock: inner `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (line 458, default 300s) and outer `_with_timeout(..., HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)` (line 550). PoC checks confirmed: `num_predict` appears only in the BULK path; `max_tokens` appears nowhere in seam.py.

Refutation attempts that failed:
1. "The SDK offers no per-call output-token cap, so an equivalent to num_predict cannot be applied." I inspected the pinned SDK (claude-agent-sdk 0.2.102, .venv). `ClaudeAgentOptions` (types.py line 1634) indeed has no `max_tokens`/`max_output_tokens` field — but it DOES expose `max_budget_usd` (line 1714: "The query will stop if this budget is exceeded") and `max_turns` (line 1708). These are available, unused guards that would bound a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation independent of wall-clock. So the asymmetry the finding describes is real and an applicable guard existed; the SDK detail only narrows the exact mechanism name, it does not moot the asymmetry.
2. "Wall-clock fully bounds it." This is exactly the argument the BULK docstring rejects (a call blocked in generation is terminated only when the full 300s elapses, consuming the entire subscription turn plus a bounded-pool worker slot for 300s, where BULK would stop at 2048 tokens well inside its window). The fail_after does cancel the subprocess, but only after the full timeout — a duration bound, not a cost/length bound.
3. No test or contract enforces a HIGH_STAKES output/cost cap; grep confirms none exists.

Caveat (not disqualifying): HIGH_STAKES is not strictly unbounded — the subprocess IS cancelled at HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT, so this is a weaker/asymmetric guard, not a total absence of any guard. The finding explicitly frames it as "bounded only by wall-clock," which is accurate, so the caveat does not invalidate it. Finding survives.

content.text:
Finding X-1 survives. I verified it against the actual code and could not disprove it.

Key file: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py`

The asymmetry is real: BULK always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 368, with the docstring at lines 95-99 explicitly arguing an output bound — not wall-clock — is "the real guard"), while HIGH_STAKES's `ClaudeAgentOptions` (lines 435-442) sets no output/cost/turn bound and is guarded only by wall-clock (`anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` line 458, plus the outer `_with_timeout` line 550).

My strongest refutation attempt — that the SDK simply offers no equivalent knob — actually failed and strengthened the finding: the pinned `claude-agent-sdk` 0.2.102 `ClaudeAgentOptions` (`.venv/.../claude_agent_sdk/types.py`) has no `max_tokens`, but it does expose `max_budget_usd` (line 1714, "the query will stop if this budget is exceeded") and `max_turns` (line 1708) — available runaway/cost guards that the seam does not use. So an applicable guard existed and was applied only to the local tier.

One honest caveat for the fixer: HIGH_STAKES is not strictly unbounded — the subprocess is cancelled at the 300s timeout — so this is a weaker/asymmetric guard rather than a total absence. The finding frames it correctly as "bounded only by wall-clock," so it stands.
